cards.title: Normal Vital Sign Ranges (Adult)
cards.body:
Pulse: 60–100 bpm
Respiratory rate: 12–20 /min
BP: <120/80 ideal
Temperature: 36.5–37.5 °C orally
SpO₂: 95–100%

Know the cut-offs, questions often hide on the borderline values.
cards.clinicalNote: Your post-op patient says they feel their heart racing. You count a pulse of 130 and BP 88/54. Don't chart it and move on. Tachycardia plus a falling BP is early shock. Escalate before the numbers get worse.

cards.title: Remember Hypertension Stages
cards.body:
JNC 8 / ACC-AHA:
• Normal: <120/<80
• Elevated: 120–129 / <80
• Stage 1: 130–139 / 80–89
• Stage 2: ≥140 / ≥90
• Crisis: >180 / >120 → emergency if end-organ damage

Memory hook: "13/8 starts the stage."

cards.title: When Vitals Are Lying
body: Patient just ran in → wait 5 min before BP
Cuff too small → falsely HIGH
Cuff too large → falsely low
Hot drink within 15 min → oral temp unreliable
Cold extremity → SpO₂ inaccurate
White-coat HTN → patient nervous in clinic
cards.clinicalNote: An anxious patient in clinic reads 158/96. Before you flag hypertension, sit them quietly for five minutes and recheck on a properly sized cuff, white-coat effect and a small cuff both push the number up falsely.

cards.title: Quick Self-Check
cards.body:
Pulse 110 in a febrile patient, primary or compensatory tachycardia?

Answer: COMPENSATORY. Each 1°C above 38.3 °C raises HR by ~10 bpm. Treat the fever, recheck the pulse.

cards.title: WHO 5 Moments of Hand Hygiene
cards.body:
1. BEFORE touching a patient
2. BEFORE clean/aseptic procedure
3. AFTER body fluid exposure risk
4. AFTER touching a patient
5. AFTER touching patient surroundings

Moments 1, 2 protect the patient. Moments 3, 4, 5 protect YOU and OTHERS.

cards.title: Levels of Asepsis
cards.body:
Cleaning < Disinfection < Sterilisation

• Cleaning: removes visible dirt
• Disinfection: kills most microbes (not always spores)
• Sterilisation: kills ALL, including spores

Spaulding classification:
• Critical (enters sterile tissue) → Sterilise
• Semi-critical (mucosa contact) → High-level disinfection
• Non-critical (skin contact) → Low-level disinfection

cards.title: PPE Donning vs Doffing
body: Don IN ORDER: Gown → Mask → Goggles → Gloves
Doff IN OPPOSITE ORDER (mostly): Gloves → Goggles → Gown → Mask
Most contaminated piece comes off first
Mask comes off LAST (highest infectious risk near face)
Hand hygiene between each step

cards.title: Device → FiO₂ → Flow
cards.body:
Nasal cannula: 1–6 L/min → ~24–44% (each 1 L adds ~4%)
Simple face mask: 5–8 L/min → ~40–60% (min 5 L to flush CO₂)
Venturi mask: precise, fixed FiO₂ (24–60%), best for COPD
Non-rebreather: 10–15 L/min → up to ~90%, emergencies

Rule: the more precise the control needed, the more you favour a Venturi.

cards.title: Oxygen Safety
body: No open flames / smoking near O₂
Humidify flows >4 L/min to prevent mucosal drying
COPD CO₂-retainers: target SpO₂ 88–92%, low flow
A non-rebreather needs the reservoir bag inflated before applying
Check tubing for kinks; assess SpO₂ and work of breathing, not the number alone

cards.title: The Cardiac Cycle in 30 Seconds
cards.body:
DIASTOLE (relaxation, filling):
• Atria contract → push final 20–30% blood into ventricles
• "Atrial kick", lost in AFib

SYSTOLE (contraction, ejection):
• AV valves CLOSE → S1 ("lub")
• Ventricles contract isovolumetrically
• Aortic & pulmonary valves open
• Blood ejects
• Semilunar valves CLOSE → S2 ("dub")

Stroke Volume = End-Diastolic − End-Systolic Volume

cards.title: Heart Sounds
cards.body:
S1 ("lub"): AV valves closing, beginning of systole
S2 ("dub"), semilunar valves closing, end of systole
S3, early diastole rapid filling, normal in young, pathological in HF (ventricular gallop)
S4, late diastole atrial kick, pathological, ventricular stiffness (atrial gallop)

Mnemonic: "Tennessee" = S4 S1 S2 (a-S4-S1-S2 = ten-ne-ssee). "Kentucky" = S1 S2 S3.

cards.title: Cardiac Output Equation
body: CO = SV × HR
Normal: ~4–8 L/min
SV depends on: Preload (filling), Afterload (resistance), Contractility
Frank-Starling Law: more stretch = stronger contraction (up to a point)
In heart failure, the curve flattens, more preload no longer helps

cards.title: ROME: Read an ABG Fast
cards.body:
Step 1 – pH: <7.35 acidosis, >7.45 alkalosis
Step 2 – ROME: Respiratory Opposite, Metabolic Equal
  • pH and CO₂ move OPPOSITE → respiratory
  • pH and HCO₃⁻ move the SAME way → metabolic
Step 3 – Compensation: is the other value shifting to push pH back to normal?

Normals: pH 7.35–7.45 · PaCO₂ 35–45 · HCO₃⁻ 22–26

cards.title: Common Causes
body: Metabolic acidosis: DKA, lactic acidosis, diarrhoea, renal failure
Metabolic alkalosis: vomiting, excess antacids, diuretics
Respiratory acidosis: hypoventilation, COPD, opioid overdose
Respiratory alkalosis: hyperventilation, anxiety, high altitude

cards.title: DKA vs HHS: How To Tell Them Apart
cards.body:
DKA (Type 1, younger, acute):
• Glucose 250–600
• Severe ketosis ↑↑
• pH < 7.3 (acidosis)
• Kussmaul breathing, fruity breath
• Onset: hours to days

HHS (Type 2, older, slower):
• Glucose >600 (often >1000)
• Minimal ketosis
• pH > 7.3 (no acidosis)
• Profound dehydration, altered mental state
• Onset: days to weeks
cards.clinicalNote: A young Type 1 diabetic arrives drowsy, breathing deeply, with fruity breath. Think DKA first: check glucose, ketones and pH. The deep 'Kussmaul' breathing is the body dumping acid. Never mistake it for simple anxiety.

cards.title: DKA Management Priorities
cards.body:
F-I-K (in that order):

FLUIDS first: NS 1L in first hour, then continue
INSULIN second, regular insulin infusion 0.1 U/kg/hr
POTASSIUM third, replenish before serum K+ drops with insulin therapy (insulin drives K+ into cells)

DO NOT give insulin first without fluids, risk of vascular collapse.
DO NOT give insulin if K+ < 3.3. Give K+ first.

cards.title: Hypoglycaemia: Rule of 15
body: Conscious patient with BG < 70: give 15g fast carbs (e.g. 3 glucose tabs, 150mL juice)
Recheck in 15 minutes
If still <70, repeat
When >70 → give a complex carb + protein snack
Unconscious: IV dextrose 25% 50mL OR IM glucagon 1 mg
NEVER give oral glucose to an unconscious patient (aspiration)

cards.title: Four Types at a Glance
cards.body:
HYPOVOLAEMIC: blood/fluid loss → ↓preload (haemorrhage, burns)
CARDIOGENIC: pump failure (MI) → ↓cardiac output, congestion
DISTRIBUTIVE: massive vasodilation (septic, anaphylactic, neurogenic) → ↓SVR, warm then cold
OBSTRUCTIVE: physical block (tamponade, tension pneumothorax, PE)

All end the same way: inadequate tissue perfusion → cellular hypoxia.

cards.title: Early Recognition & Priorities
body: Early signs: tachycardia, restlessness, cool clammy skin, narrowing pulse pressure
Late signs: hypotension, weak pulse, oliguria (<30 mL/hr), altered consciousness
Position: flat with legs raised (except cardiogenic → semi-Fowler)
Hypovolaemic: stop bleeding + IV fluids; Anaphylactic: IM adrenaline first
Monitor urine output: the most sensitive bedside perfusion marker

cards.title: The "LASA" Look-Alike Sound-Alike Drugs
cards.body:
Common dangerous confusions:
• Hydralazine vs Hydroxyzine
• Cefazolin vs Ceftazidime
• Furosemide vs Torsemide
• Insulin Humalog vs Humulin
• Digoxin vs Doxepin

Defences: read label THREE times (pulling, preparing, giving), use generic names, "tall man" lettering (HumALOG vs HumULIN), independent double-checks for high-alert drugs.
cards.clinicalNote: You're handed an order for 'hydroxyzine' but the chart context screams cardiac. Stop: hydralazine and hydroxyzine are a classic LASA pair. Read back the indication, not just the name, before you give it.

cards.title: "High-Alert" Categories: APINCH
cards.body:
A: Anticoagulants (heparin, warfarin)
P. Potassium concentrate (NEVER push IV undiluted, fatal!)
I: Insulin
N: Narcotics (opioids)
C. Chemotherapy / Cytotoxics
H. Heparin specifically (often listed twice)

These have the highest harm potential when given wrong. Always double-check.

cards.title: Drug → Antidote (Must-Know)
body: Heparin → Protamine sulphate (monitor aPTT)
Warfarin → Vitamin K (+ FFP/PCC if urgent; monitor INR)
Opioids → Naloxone
Benzodiazepines → Flumazenil
Paracetamol → N-acetylcysteine
Digoxin → Digoxin-specific Fab fragments
Iron → Desferrioxamine
Organophosphates → Atropine + Pralidoxime
Methotrexate → Folinic acid (leucovorin)

cards.title: High-Alert Drugs: A PINCH
cards.body:
A – Anticoagulants (heparin, warfarin)
P – Potassium (NEVER IV push undiluted, fatal)
I – Insulin
N – Narcotics (opioids)
C – Chemotherapy / cytotoxics
H – Heparin

These carry the highest harm if given wrong, independent double-check.

cards.title: India UIP Schedule (Quick View)
cards.body:
BIRTH: BCG + OPV-0 + HepB-0
6 WEEKS: Penta-1 + OPV-1 + Rota-1 + fIPV-1 + PCV-1
10 WEEKS: Penta-2 + OPV-2 + Rota-2
14 WEEKS: Penta-3 + OPV-3 + Rota-3 + fIPV-2 + PCV-2
9 MONTHS: MR-1 + JE-1 (endemic) + PCV booster + Vit A
16–24 M: DPT-1 booster + OPV booster + MR-2 + JE-2
5–6 YR: DPT-2 booster
10 YR & 16 YR: Td

cards.title: Live vs Inactivated Vaccines
cards.body:
LIVE (don't give in pregnancy / severe immunosuppression):
• MMR, MR
• BCG
• OPV
• Varicella
• Yellow fever
• Rotavirus
• Typhoid (oral Ty21a)

INACTIVATED / SUBUNIT (safer):
• DPT, Hib, HepB, IPV
• Influenza (injectable)
• PCV, HPV
• Rabies, JE
• Typhoid (injectable)

cards.title: General Danger Signs in a Sick Child
cards.body:
Refer URGENTLY if a child:
• Is unable to drink or breastfeed
• Vomits everything
• Has had convulsions
• Is lethargic or unconscious

IMNCI uses a colour triage: PINK = urgent referral, YELLOW = treat at facility, GREEN = home care/advice.
cards.clinicalNote: A mother brings a toddler who 'won't drink and vomits everything'. That single sentence is two IMNCI danger signs. Don't wait for a fever spike, danger signs alone mean urgent referral.

cards.title: Fast Breathing Cut-offs (Pneumonia)
body: <2 months: ≥ 60 breaths/min
2–12 months: ≥ 50 breaths/min
12 months–5 years: ≥ 40 breaths/min
Chest indrawing = severe pneumonia → refer
Count breaths for a FULL minute when the child is calm

cards.title: Four Stages of Labour
cards.body:
STAGE 1: Onset of regular contractions → full cervical dilatation (10 cm).
  • Latent phase: 0 → ~6 cm
  • Active phase: 6 → 10 cm (~1 cm/hr primigravida)

STAGE 2: Full dilatation → birth of baby.
  • Cardinal movements happen here

STAGE 3: Birth of baby → expulsion of placenta (typically <30 min)

STAGE 4: 1–2 hr observation post-placenta. Watch for PPH

cards.title: Cardinal Movements (Mechanism of Labour)
cards.body:
Every Decent Family Is Extremely Rich, Earning Excellent Eternal Riches

• Engagement
• Descent
• Flexion
• Internal rotation
• Extension
• Restitution
• External rotation
• Expulsion

These describe how the foetal head navigates the pelvic canal.

cards.title: The Four Stages
cards.body:
FIRST: onset of true labour → full dilatation (10 cm)
  • Latent (0–3 cm) then active (4–10 cm)
SECOND: full dilatation → birth of the baby
THIRD: birth of baby → delivery of placenta (watch for the 3 signs of separation)
FOURTH: ~1 hr after the placenta. Immediate recovery, watch for PPH.

cards.title: Partograph Essentials
body: Plots cervical dilatation against time to spot abnormal/obstructed labour early
Alert line: 1 cm/hr in active phase; crossing it prompts review
Action line: 4 hr right of the alert line → intervention
Also tracks fetal heart rate, contractions, descent, liquor
A simple tool that prevents prolonged/obstructed labour deaths

cards.title: Mortality Indicators (India approx.)
cards.body:
IMR (Infant Mortality Rate): ~28 per 1000 live births
NMR (Neonatal): ~20 per 1000 live births
U5MR (Under-5): ~32 per 1000 live births
MMR (Maternal): ~97 per 100,000 live births

Key definition trick. IMR/NMR/U5MR use /1000 LIVE BIRTHS. MMR uses /100,000 LIVE BIRTHS. Crude death rate uses /1000 POPULATION.

cards.title: Levels of Prevention: Quick Mapping
cards.body:
PRIMORDIAL → eliminate the risk factor (e.g., healthy lifestyle in whole society)
PRIMARY → prevent disease before onset (immunisation, sanitation)
SECONDARY → early detection in asymptomatic (Pap smear, BP screening)
TERTIARY → limit disability after disease (rehab, dialysis)

cards.title: Key Programmes & Their Focus
cards.body:
RNTCP / NTEP – Tuberculosis (DOTS)
NVBDCP – Vector-borne (malaria, dengue, filaria)
NLEP – Leprosy
RCH / RMNCH+A – Reproductive & child health
UIP – Universal Immunisation
NPCDCS – Non-communicable diseases (diabetes, cancer, stroke)
NMHP – Mental health

cards.title: Quick Facts
body: DOTS = Directly Observed Treatment, Short-course, cornerstone of TB control
India aims to ELIMINATE TB ahead of the global 2030 SDG target
ASHA = Accredited Social Health Activist, the village-level link worker
Cold chain keeps vaccines viable from manufacturer to child
Sub-centre is the first/most peripheral contact point in rural health

cards.title: Therapeutic vs Non-Therapeutic Responses
cards.body:
THERAPEUTIC:
• Active listening
• Open-ended questions
• Reflection ("You feel scared right now")
• Silence (let them speak)
• Validation ("Your feelings make sense")

AVOID:
• False reassurance ("Everything will be fine")
• Giving advice ("You should…")
• "Why" questions (feel accusatory)
• Changing the subject
• Minimising ("It's not that bad")
• Approval/disapproval

cards.title: Suicide Risk: SAD PERSONS Scale
cards.body:
S: Sex (male higher)
A. Age (<19 or >45)
D. Depression
P. Previous attempts
E. Ethanol/drug abuse
R. Rational thinking loss
S. Social support lacking
O. Organised plan
N. No spouse
S. Sickness (chronic illness)

0–4 low, 5–6 moderate, 7–10 high risk. ASK directly: it does not plant ideas.

cards.title: Recognise Them in a Vignette
cards.body:
Denial – refusing to accept reality
Repression – unconscious blocking of painful thoughts
Projection – attributing your feelings to others
Displacement – redirecting feelings to a safer target
Rationalisation – logical excuses for behaviour
Regression – reverting to earlier behaviour under stress
Reaction formation – acting opposite to true feelings
Sublimation (mature) – channelling impulses into acceptable activity

cards.title: Exam Tips
body: Sublimation is the only one considered consistently "mature/healthy"
Displacement vs projection: displacement REDIRECTS the feeling; projection ASSIGNS it to someone else
Regression is common in hospitalised children (bed-wetting, thumb-sucking)
Identify by the behaviour described, not the label the patient uses

cards.title: Which Method For Which Item?
cards.body:
AUTOCLAVE (121°C, 15 psi, 15 min): surgical instruments, dressings, fluids, glassware
DRY HEAT (160°C / 2 hr): glassware, oils, powders, sharp instruments (won't blunt)
ETHYLENE OXIDE: heat-sensitive items (endoscopes, plastics)
FORMALDEHYDE / GLUTARALDEHYDE: heat-sensitive equipment, surfaces
RADIATION (gamma): single-use plastics, sutures, commercial items
FILTRATION: heat-sensitive liquids (serum, vaccines)

cards.title: Airborne: "My Chicken Tastes Tasty"
cards.body:
AIRBORNE (droplet nuclei <5 µm, negative-pressure room + N95):
  • Measles
  • Tuberculosis
  • Varicella (chickenpox) / disseminated zoster

DROPLET (>5 µm, surgical mask, ~1 m): influenza, pertussis, meningococcus, mumps, rubella
CONTACT (gown + gloves): MRSA, C. difficile, scabies

C. difficile and norovirus need SOAP-AND-WATER (alcohol rub doesn't kill spores).

cards.title: Standard Precautions
body: Apply to ALL patients regardless of diagnosis
Hand hygiene is the single most effective measure
PPE chosen by anticipated exposure
Don: gown → mask → goggles → gloves; Doff: gloves → goggles → gown → mask
Most contaminated item comes off first; hand hygiene between steps

cards.title: Renal Diet Cheat Sheet
cards.body:
PRE-DIALYSIS CKD: limit protein (~0.6–0.8 g/kg), restrict K+, phosphate, sodium, fluid

ON HAEMODIALYSIS: INCREASE protein (1.2 g/kg) because dialysis removes it. Still restrict K+, phosphate, sodium, fluid.

FOODS HIGH IN K+ to avoid: bananas, oranges, tomato, coconut water, leafy greens, potatoes (soak before cooking helps)
FOODS HIGH IN PHOSPHATE: dairy, nuts, processed foods, cola

Fluid limit on dialysis: ~1L/day + previous day's urine output

cards.title: Fat- vs Water-Soluble
body: Fat-soluble = "ADEK" (A, D, E, K), stored in body, toxicity possible
Water-soluble = B-complex + C, not stored, deficiency appears faster
Vitamin A: night blindness, Bitot's spots
Vitamin D: rickets (child) / osteomalacia (adult)
Vitamin K: bleeding (clotting factors II, VII, IX, X)

cards.title: B-Complex Deficiencies
body: B1 (Thiamine): beriberi, Wernicke encephalopathy
B2 (Riboflavin): angular stomatitis, glossitis
B3 (Niacin): pellagra: the 3 Ds (Dermatitis, Diarrhoea, Dementia)
B6 (Pyridoxine): peripheral neuropathy (esp. with isoniazid)
B9/B12: megaloblastic anaemia; B12 also causes neuro signs
Vitamin C: scurvy (bleeding gums, poor wound healing)

cards.title: What this tests
cards.body: Number series questions test whether you can spot the rule linking a sequence of numbers and use it to predict the next term. It is pattern recognition under time pressure, not heavy calculation.

cards.title: Step-by-step method
body: Write the gaps between consecutive terms, the first differences.
If the gaps are equal, the rule is +constant (arithmetic). If they grow or shrink steadily, look at the second differences.
If differences don’t settle, test ratios (×constant), squares/cubes, or two interleaved series.
Apply the confirmed rule to the last term, then check it fits the whole series.

cards.title: Worked example
cards.body:
Series: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ?

Step 1: First differences: 4, 6, 8, 10.
Step 2: The gaps rise by 2 each time, so the next gap is 12.
Step 3: Confirmed rule: add an increasing even number (equivalently term = n(n+1)).
Step 4: 30 + 12 = 42.

Answer: 42.

cards.title: Common mistake to avoid
cards.body: Don’t grab the first pattern you see and stop. Always extend your rule back across the whole series to confirm it fits every term, not just the last two.

cards.title: What this tests
cards.body: These test your ability to track family links from a worded statement and work out how two people are related, often through a chain of ‘the son of’, ‘the only daughter of’ phrases.

cards.title: Step-by-step method
body: Read the sentence from the innermost phrase outward, resolve the smallest relation first.
Translate each phrase into a quick family tree (+ for male, − for female, lines for parent/child).
Substitute each resolved person back into the outer phrase, step by step.
State the final relation from the asked person’s point of view, watching the gender.

cards.title: Worked example
cards.body:
‘His mother is the only daughter of my mother’. How is the speaker (a woman) related to him?

Step 1: Innermost: ‘the only daughter of my mother’.
Step 2: The only daughter of the speaker’s mother is the speaker herself.
Step 3: So ‘his mother’ = the speaker.
Step 4: She is his mother.

Answer: Mother.

cards.title: Common mistake to avoid
cards.body: The biggest trap is assuming the speaker’s gender or skipping ‘only’. ‘Only daughter’ forces that daughter to be the speaker herself, read those qualifiers literally.

cards.title: What this tests
cards.body: Direction questions test whether you can track turns and distances on a mental map, then find either the final direction or the straight-line distance from the start.

cards.title: Step-by-step method
body: Draw axes: North up, South down, East right, West left. Start at the origin.
Plot each leg. ‘Turn right’ rotates your facing 90° clockwise; ‘turn left’ 90° anticlockwise.
Total the movement along each axis separately, net North–South and net East–West.
For distance, use Pythagoras on the two net legs; opposite legs of equal length cancel.

cards.title: Worked example
cards.body:
Walk 5 km N, turn right 3 km, turn right 5 km. Distance from start?

Step 1: Origin, facing North.
Step 2: 5 km North; right → East 3 km; right → South 5 km.
Step 3: North 5 and South 5 cancel; net East = 3.
Step 4: One leg remains, so distance = 3 km.

Answer: 3 km.

cards.title: Common mistake to avoid
cards.body: Confusing ‘distance walked’ with ‘distance from start’. The question almost always wants displacement, cancel opposite legs before measuring.

cards.title: What this tests
cards.body: These test whether you can find the rule that turns a word into its code, usually a fixed letter shift, a reversal, or a position swap, and then apply the same rule to a new word.

cards.title: Step-by-step method
body: Line up the original word and its code, letter by letter.
Find the change per letter: count the alphabet gap (C→D is +1) and check it is constant.
Watch for variations: alternating shifts, reversed order, or position-based shifts.
Apply the confirmed rule to each letter of the target word independently.

cards.title: Worked example
cards.body:
If CAT = DBU, then DOG = ?

Step 1: Align: C→D, A→B, T→U.
Step 2: Each letter moves +1 in the alphabet.
Step 3: Rule confirmed: shift every letter forward by one.
Step 4: D→E, O→P, G→H.

Answer: EPH.

cards.title: Common mistake to avoid
cards.body: Forgetting to apply the rule to every letter, students shift the first letter then copy the rest. Process each letter on its own.

cards.title: What this tests
cards.body: Syllogisms test whether a conclusion necessarily follows from the given statements, pure logic, not real-world knowledge. ‘Necessarily’ is the key word.

cards.title: Step-by-step method
body: Treat each statement as a set: ‘All A are B’ means A sits entirely inside B.
Sketch overlapping circles (a Venn diagram) for the statements.
A conclusion is valid only if it holds in EVERY possible diagram, not just one.
Reject any conclusion that could be false in some valid arrangement. ‘some’ and ‘all’ must be exact.

cards.title: Worked example
cards.body:
All cats are animals. All animals breathe. Conclusion?

Step 1: Cats sit inside Animals.
Step 2: Animals sit inside ‘things that breathe’.
Step 3: So Cats sit inside ‘things that breathe’ in every diagram.
Step 4: ‘All cats breathe’ always holds.

Answer: All cats breathe.

cards.title: Common mistake to avoid
cards.body: Bringing in outside knowledge, or accepting a conclusion that only ‘could’ be true. If you can draw one valid diagram where it fails, it does not follow.

cards.title: What this tests
cards.body: These test the idea of a work rate, how much of a job someone does per day, and how rates add when people work together.

cards.title: Step-by-step method
body: Convert each worker’s time into a one-day rate: finish in n days → 1/n per day.
Add the rates of everyone working together.
Invert the combined rate to get the combined time.
For staggered work, multiply rate × days for each phase and sum the work done.

cards.title: Worked example
cards.body:
A finishes in 10 days, B in 15 days. Together?

Step 1: Rates: A = 1/10, B = 1/15 per day.
Step 2: Combined = 1/10 + 1/15 = 3/30 + 2/30 = 5/30.
Step 3: 5/30 = 1/6 per day.
Step 4: Time = 1 ÷ (1/6) = 6 days.

Answer: 6 days.

cards.title: Common mistake to avoid
cards.body: Averaging the two times, e.g. (10+15)/2. Work rates add, times do not. Always go via 1/n per day.

cards.title: What this tests
cards.body: Percentage questions test conversion between percentages and fractions, and how successive percentage changes combine, a frequent NORCET trap.

cards.title: Step-by-step method
body: Turn a percentage into a multiplier: +20% means ×1.20, −20% means ×0.80.
For successive changes, multiply the multipliers in order. Never add the percentages.
Compare the final multiplier to 1: above 1 is a net gain, below 1 a net loss.
Convert the final multiplier back into a percentage change.

cards.title: Worked example
cards.body:
A value rises 20%, then falls 20%. Net change?

Step 1: +20% → ×1.20; −20% → ×0.80.
Step 2: Combined = 1.20 × 0.80 = 0.96.
Step 3: 0.96 is below 1, so it is a net loss.
Step 4: 0.96 = 96% of the original → 4% decrease.

Answer: 4% decrease.

cards.title: Common mistake to avoid
cards.body: Assuming equal up-and-down percentages cancel to zero. They act on different bases, so the result is always a small net decrease.

cards.title: What this tests
cards.body: These test whether you can scale and combine ratios, especially chaining two ratios that share a common term.

cards.title: Step-by-step method
body: Write each ratio clearly and identify the term they share.
Scale both ratios so the shared term has the same value (use the LCM).
Read the combined ratio across all terms.
Pick out the two terms the question asks for and simplify.

cards.title: Worked example
cards.body:
A : B = 2 : 3 and B : C = 4 : 5. Find A : C.

Step 1: Shared term is B (3 in the first, 4 in the second).
Step 2: LCM of 3 and 4 is 12: scale to A : B = 8 : 12 and B : C = 12 : 15.
Step 3: Combined A : B : C = 8 : 12 : 15.
Step 4: A : C = 8 : 15.

Answer: 8 : 15.

cards.title: Common mistake to avoid
cards.body: Equalise the shared term before reading across. Blindly multiplying across ratios is unreliable, make B common first.
